title: Chapter 6: Blackout
content: The Hollow smelled worse than Aline remembered.

Bourbon soaked into wood that had never been properly cleaned, sweat from bodies pressed too close in a room too small, the faint chemical bite of cheap disinfectant that never quite masked either. She'd performed here for fourteen months and never once stopped noticing the smell. It coated the back of her throat like a film.
Tonight, there were twice as many bodies to make it worse.

She stood in the narrow hallway behind the bar — the one that led to the bathroom and the storage closet she'd used as a green room since the beginning — and stared through the gap in the doorframe at the crowd crammed inside. The Hollow held maybe ninety people on a good night. There had to be a hundred and fifty in there, maybe more. Shoulder to shoulder. Phones already out, flashbulbs popping like static lightning. The Thursday night regulars elbowed for space beside strangers in nice coats who'd clearly never been south of 14th Street, let alone inside a dive that watered its well whiskey.
Word got around. The viral singer started here.

Aline touched the neck of her guitar case without opening it. She wasn't playing guitar tonight. She was singing with the house backing track — Derek's idea, because "people want the full experience, sweetheart, give them the full experience." She didn't argue. One more show. Her contract required one more show, and then she was free of Derek Vance's roster, free of Thursday nights in a room that smelled like drunk men's regrets, free of tips split sixty-forty in a direction that never favored her.
One more show.
"Aline!"

Derek pushed through the crowd from behind the bar, and for a moment she barely recognized him. He was smiling — not the thin, compressed thing he usually wore, but a wide, loose grin that pulled at the corners of his eyes. He'd buttoned his shirt higher than normal. His gold pinky ring caught the light and threw it back in little sparks as he gestured broadly at the packed room.

"Look at this! Look at this, sweetheart." He grabbed her by the shoulder — his grip too familiar, his palm damp — and steered her toward the bar. "Biggest night this place has ever seen. And it's because of you."

Because of the internet. Because of a song she'd recorded on a shoebox microphone and uploaded at three in the morning. But she nodded, because that was what you did with Derek. You nodded, you smiled, you got through it.

"Listen." He leaned in. Bourbon on his breath, sour and warm. "After the set, we celebrate. I got a bottle of the good stuff. Just you and me. Toast to your big break." He patted her shoulder twice, his pinky ring dragging cold across her collarbone, and then he was gone — back behind the bar, pouring fast, working the room with an ease she'd never seen in him before.
Aline watched him go. Something in his voice. Just you and me.
She filed it away and walked toward the stage.

The stage was a corner of the room where the floor was raised four inches and a mic stand stood next to a water-stained stool. No curtain. No backdrop. Just a spotlight with a bad bulb that buzzed like a trapped insect. She stepped up, adjusted the microphone, and the crowd roared.

Not for her. Not really. For the idea of her — the woman from the video, the overnight sensation, the story they could tell at parties. *I saw her before she was anybody.* But when she opened her mouth, that stopped mattering.
The first note left her throat and the room changed.

It always changed. That was the thing eleven years of almost-invisibility had taught her: the voice was the one weapon she had that never failed. Even in a dive bar with acoustic ceiling tiles that swallowed midrange and a crowd that talked through every other act, her voice filled the space like water filling a vessel — finding every crack, every corner, pressing against every surface until there was nowhere left for sound to hide and nothing left for anyone to do but listen.

She sang her set. Old songs, new songs, the one that had gone viral. The crowd recorded her with shaking hands. Someone near the back cried. Aline felt it — that electricity, that thread of connection running from her chest to two hundred eyes, the thing she'd chased across every empty open mic and every audition room that ended in *we'll call you.* She was not invisible tonight. For the first time in her life, she was the gravitational center of a room, and the feeling was so vast and so sudden that she almost couldn't hold it.
Between the fourth and fifth songs, while the crowd was still clapping, she glanced toward the bar.

Derek was behind the counter. He had two champagne flutes out — the fancy ones he kept on the top shelf for occasions that never came. He was pouring from a bottle of something that looked expensive, the label turned away from her. But the pour for the second glass didn't come from the bottle.

His hand dipped into his pocket. A small vial emerged — glass, clear, no bigger than a pinky finger. He tilted it into one of the flutes. Just a few drops. Then he tucked the vial away and picked up both glasses, setting them behind the register.
The clapping faded. The next song started in her head but not in her throat.
She knew what she'd seen.
The last song ended. The crowd erupted. Aline let the applause wash over her and then stepped down from the stage, every muscle in her body tight, her eyes fixed on Derek.
He was already moving toward her, both glasses in hand, champagne catching the bad light in pale gold streams. He held one out.
"To the Lark." His smile was enormous. Practiced. His eyes were bright with something that wasn't pride.
She didn't take the glass.
"I'm good, thanks."

The smile held, but the skin around it tightened. A tremor at the jaw. "It's just champagne, sweetheart. Don't be rude on your big night." He pushed the glass toward her. His fingers wrapped around the stem of the other flute — the one he'd poured for himself, untouched by whatever was in his pocket.

The liquid in her glass was wrong. Too clear. Slightly viscous, the way oil hangs on the surface of water. The champagne bubbles rose through it differently — slower, catching against something that shouldn't have been there. Her stomach turned.
"I said I'm good, Derek."
"Sweetheart—"
And then the lights went out.

Not a flicker. Not a fade. Instant. Absolute. As if someone had cut the throat of the building itself. The music died. The neon beer signs behind the bar — gone. The buzzing spotlight — gone. The exit signs, the emergency strips, the little red glow of the smoke detector, the green power lights on the sound board — all of it, every single source of illumination, swallowed whole.
The crowd screamed.

Aline couldn't see her own hand in front of her face. The darkness was so total, so complete, that it had texture — a physical weight pressing against her eyes. She could feel the air thicken with the heat and stink of panicked bodies, bourbon and sweat and fear-blunt adrenaline flooding the room. Someone's shoulder crashed into hers. A glass shattered somewhere to her left, the sound impossibly sharp in the sightless chaos, each fragment registering like a fingernail snapping. She could taste the panic — metallic, electric, coating her tongue the way the bourbon smell coated everything else.

The backup generator should have kicked in. The emergency lights should have flooded the exits red. None of it happened. Nothing happened. Just black. Just the roar of the crowd dissolving into the black.
And then — sound.
Not from the stage. Not from any single point. From everywhere.

Every speaker in the bar — the PA, the house system, the little Bluetooth unit behind the tap — every phone still powered in the room, every digital device with a speaker and a charge, emitted the same voice at the same time. Low. Calm. The calm of a blade resting against a throat.
**"Derek."**
The screaming stopped. A hundred and fifty people went silent, because the voice was inside their phones, inside their ears, inside the walls.
**"If you touch that glass to her lips, your funeral will be livestreamed on TikTok. I will ruin every trace of your existence. Financial. Social. Legal. Gone in forty-eight hours."**
A pause. The silence in it was suffocating.
**"Leave her alone."**
The lights came back.

All of them. Every bulb, every sign, every screen, every phone — blazing to life at full intensity, white and harsh and searing after the absolute black. Aline threw her arm over her eyes. The crowd was chaos — people clutching each other, people filming on phones that should have died in the blackout but hadn't, phones that somehow still held full battery, phones that had all spoken with one voice.
Derek stood three feet from her.

He was white. Not pale — white. The color of paper, of bone, of men who have heard their own death sentence spoken from the mouth of God. Sweat plastered his hair to his forehead. His eyes were wild, darting from face to face, corner to corner, searching for the person the voice belonged to. His hands were empty. The champagne flute lay in pieces on the sticky floor — shattered, the viscous liquid pooled among the glass like something that had been alive and was now very much not. He looked small. Pathetic. A man who had reached for control and found himself grasped by something so far above him that he couldn't even comprehend its shape.
"Who said that?" His voice cracked. "WHO SAID THAT?"
No one answered.

Because no one in the room had said it. It had come from everywhere and nowhere. From the walls. From the phones in their hands. From the speaker in the ceiling that hadn't worked since 2019. Every device. Every speaker. Every light.
Aline stared at the broken glass on the floor. At the strange, slow puddle spreading from it. At Derek's ashen face.
She thought: *He was going to drug me.*
She thought: *Something just saved me.*
She thought: *What the hell just saved me?*

The crowd was surging toward the exit, phones raised, a dozen livestreams already broadcasting the aftermath of whatever had just happened. Derek was still spinning, still shouting — "WHO WAS IT? WHO THE F*** WAS IT?" — but his voice was thin and small and nobody was listening. He'd lost the room. He'd lost control of everything in it. He was a man standing in the wreckage of his own plan, sweating and pale and utterly, completely powerless.
Aline backed away from him. Her hand hit the wall. The wall was cold against her palm. Everything was cold. She hadn't noticed the sweat on her own skin until the air hit it.
Her phone buzzed.
She looked down. Unknown number. The caller ID was blank — not blocked, not private, just empty, as if the number existed in a space where numbers weren't supposed to be.
She answered with trembling hands. The phone was cold too — cold against her ear, cold against her cheek.
"Are you hurt?"

Cass's voice. Softer than she'd ever heard it. Stripped of the digital fuzz, the ironic distance, the deliberate performance of control. Just a voice, low and careful, asking a question that sounded like it was carrying the weight of something enormous. The most human line he'd ever spoken, and it was three words.
She almost laughed. Almost cried. The two impulses crashed together in her chest and came out as a sound she didn't recognize — half breath, half sob.
"He was going to —"
"I know." No hesitation. No surprise. "I was watching."
The words landed like stones dropped into deep water. Ripples spreading out and out and out.
*I was watching.*

Not *I saw what happened.* Not *I figured it out.* *I was watching.* Present continuous. Ongoing. He had been watching — the bar, the crowd, Derek's hands, the vial, the glass, her. All of it. In real time. The full godlike scope of it crashed over her in a wave: every device, every speaker, every light, every phone. He had controlled all of it. Simultaneously. Instantly. Not a hack. Not a glitch. Something else. Something for which she did not have a word.

She should say thank you. He had just saved her from something she didn't want to finish imagining. She should say you're insane, because he had just demonstrated a capability that belonged in a military briefing, not in a bar on a Thursday night, and he had used it because he was watching her. Always watching her.
She said both.
"Thank you." A breath. "You're insane."
A pause. In it, she could hear nothing — no background noise, no ambient hum, just silence so complete it felt like he'd muted the whole world to hear her answer.
Then, almost inaudibly: "I know."
The line went dead.

Aline stood with the phone pressed to her ear long after the silence settled. The crowd was gone now, trickled out into the street to upload their footage and argue about what they'd witnessed. Derek was nowhere to be seen — probably in the back, probably shaking, probably already calculating how to spin this. The broken glass was still on the floor, glittering in the restored light like tiny, cruel stars.
She lowered the phone. Her reflection stared back from the dead screen — pale, wide-eyed, the face of a woman who had just learned two things at once:
She was protected.
She was being watched.
And she couldn't decide which one terrified her more.
